facts_disclosed: Moved office to KAFD (King Abdullah Financial District)

turns.content: Something wonderful has happened. I am sitting in my new studio right now, and for the first time in the history of this firm, the space we work in matches the work we produce.
That sounds like a significant moment. Congratulations! Where is the new studio, and what makes it feel like such a leap forward?

We have moved to KAFD — King Abdullah Financial District. The space is on the seventh floor of one of the newer towers, and the light is extraordinary. Floor-to-ceiling glazing on two sides, north and east exposure, which means we get beautiful morning light without the punishing afternoon sun. For a design studio, light is not a luxury. It is the primary tool.

North and east exposure is ideal for a design studio — you get consistent, even light for most of the working day without the colour distortion from direct southern or western sun. That matters enormously when you're evaluating materials, fabrics, and colour samples. KAFD is also a statement in terms of address — it positions the firm in the financial and corporate heart of Riyadh. Was that part of the thinking?

Entirely. A design firm's studio is its first project — the first space a client experiences before we have shown them a single rendering or material board. If our studio does not embody the quality and sensibility of our work, we are undermining our own argument before it begins. KAFD communicates that Al-Rashidi Interiors operates at a certain level. The architecture of the district itself is serious, considered, contemporary. It is the right context.

That's exactly right — your studio is your portfolio in spatial form. A client walking into a beautifully considered studio has already been convinced of something before you've said a word. How did you approach designing your own space? I imagine designing for yourself brings a different kind of pressure.

It was the most difficult project I have ever undertaken, and I mean that sincerely. When you design for a client, there is a brief, a set of constraints, a personality to interpret. When you design for yourself, you are the brief. Every choice is a declaration of who you are and what you value. There is nowhere to hide. I kept returning to one word: proportion. The ceiling height is generous — four metres — and I left it exposed in the main studio area. Concrete soffit, visible services, but everything aligned and ordered. It is not industrial in the careless way that word is often used. It is simply honest about the structure of the building.

Four-metre ceilings with exposed concrete soffit — that's a real volume to work with. At that height, the exposure reads as generosity and clarity rather than as a budget decision. Exposed services become visual noise when they're chaotic, but when they're organized, they become part of the composition. What about the material palette for the rest of the space?

The floors are poured concrete, polished to a warm grey. The walls are a lime plaster — a creamy white with just enough texture to absorb light rather than reflect it harshly. My meeting table is a single slab of walnut on a blackened steel base. The material library has open shelving in natural oak. Every surface in this studio is something I would specify for a client. There is no compromise material anywhere.

No compromise materials means every surface is a credential. When a client picks up a stone sample and then looks at your floor and sees the same level of consideration, the message is seamless. The lime plaster is a particularly strong choice — it has a warmth and depth that painted drywall simply cannot achieve. How has the team responded to the new space?

They are working differently. I noticed it within the first week. The energy is sharper. People are more careful — not in a fearful way, but in the way that a beautiful space makes you want to rise to it. My senior designer told me she finally feels like the studio reflects what she tells people she does for a living. That meant more to me than I expected.

That's one of the most meaningful things a team member can say — that the workspace validates the work. When there's a gap between the quality of what you produce and where you produce it, there's a cognitive dissonance that subtly undermines confidence. Closing that gap doesn't just improve client perception; it changes how your team sees themselves. The fact that it happened within the first week tells you the previous space was holding them back in ways that were probably invisible until the contrast appeared.

That is exactly it. I did not fully understand how much the old space was costing us — not in rent, but in ambition. We were producing work that exceeded our environment, and there is a ceiling to how long you can sustain that contradiction. Now the environment leads, and the work follows it upward. KAFD is where Al-Rashidi Interiors was always meant to be. It simply took time to arrive.

That's beautifully put — the space leading and the work following. Environment shapes aspiration in ways that are hard to quantify but impossible to ignore. And 'it simply took time to arrive' suggests this wasn't a sudden decision but the culmination of years of building toward a level where this studio made sense. Congratulations, genuinely. A studio that finally matches the calibre of the work is a milestone that changes everything that comes after it.
